original_hypothesis.title: Permanent distribution lock if a single recipient/token transfer reverts (blacklist/pause/nonstandard ERC20), blocking all transfers/mints/burns
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: distribute() sets LockedForDistribution=true in _beginDistribution(), then performs external ERC20 `transfer()` calls inside nested loops. If any transfer reverts (e.g., token is paused/blacklists a holder; token is nonstandard and reverts on ABI decoding; insufficient balance due to rebasing), the entire distribute() call reverts and the distribution remains locked (LockedForDistribution stays true because state changes prior to revert are rolled back to pre-call, but the lock can still be re-entered and get stuck across attempts if every attempt fails at the same holder/token pair). Practically, if one holder cannot receive a given distributable token, finishing a distribution may be impossible, freezing all ERC20 transfers due to the lock requirement in _beforeTokenTransfer().
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Real-world stablecoins (USDT/USDC) can blacklist addresses; many tokens can be paused. Any single problematic recipient or misconfigured distributable token can brick distributions and, by design, block all transfers/mints/burns until distribution completes.
deployment_assumptions: At least one distributableERC20 either (a) reverts on transfer for some recipients (blacklist/pause) or (b) is nonstandard (no boolean return) causing revert
At least one such recipient exists in holders[] and is approved at time of transfer attempt
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Project documentation states distributable tokens include vetted stablecoins (e.g., USDC, DAI, USDT) but no on-chain enforcement of token compliance; distribute() uses raw IERC20.transfer() without SafeERC20.

proof_of_signal.pattern: distribute(): nested loop calls `IERC20(distributableERC20s[j]).transfer(recipient, entitlement)` with no SafeERC20 wrapper; distribution lock blocks all transfers via _beforeTokenTransfer()

preconditions: LockedForDistribution can be entered (MinDistributionPeriod elapsed, supply>0, holders>0)
At least one (recipient, token) pair causes `transfer()` to revert consistently
No admin emergency-unlock function exists (none in code)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external token transfers inside distribution loop without per-recipient error handling; hard global lock on token movements while distribution in progress
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: one bad recipient/token can prevent completion forever, freezing ERC20 operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No try/catch around transfer, no skip-on-revert strategy, no admin forced unlock

analysis_questions: Is LockedForDistribution ever set true and persisted across calls, or always reverted on failing transfers (confirm state rollback behavior and whether partial progress can be resumed)?
Can distribution be resumed after revert, or does it always fail at the same index because nextDistributionRecipient doesn't advance?
What happens if a token returns false vs reverts vs no return value under Solidity 0.8.12 ABI expectations?
validation_requirements: Simulate a distributable token that reverts for one recipient; ensure distribute() cannot progress past that recipient
Test with a no-return ERC20 mock (USDT-like) to see if it reverts and bricks distributions
Confirm that mint/burn/transfer are blocked while LockedForDistribution is true and that there is no recovery path

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker ensures their address is in holders[] (and allowlisted) then triggers conditions where transfer to them reverts for a chosen distributable token (e.g., by getting blacklisted on that token, or token is paused). Anyone calling distribute() will always revert at the attacker’s index, making it impossible to complete distributions. Because transfers/mints/burns are blocked during distribution, the ERC20 becomes effectively frozen whenever a distribution attempt is made and cannot be completed.
prerequisites: Presence of a restrictive/blacklisting/pausable distributable token OR nonstandard ERC20 added to distributableERC20s

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol-wide freeze of ERC20 transferability and supply operations; distributions cannot be completed so revenue cannot be paid out
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All ERC20 holders and protocol operators
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational only (change distributableERC20s, remove holder, or migrate), but may be blocked by lock/logic depending on exact failure mode

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No negative tests for blacklisted recipients, paused tokens, or nonstandard ERC20 return values during distribute().

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Not primary; failure is deterministic revert
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block; does not require transaction ordering
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable repeated reverts at same distribution index; LockedForDistribution toggling behavior to verify

validation_result.mechanism: Unhandled revert in distribute() transfer loop causes permanent distribution lock
validation_result.impact_summary: A single reverting ERC20 transfer in distribute() (from blacklisting, token pause, or nonstandard return value) can permanently lock all ERC20 transfers, mints, and burns by leaving LockedForDistribution=true with no way to advance past the failing holder/token pair.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The distribute() function iterates over holders and performs IERC20.transfer() calls without try/catch or SafeERC20. If any transfer reverts (rather than returning false), the entire distribute() transaction reverts. Two critical failure modes exist:

1. **Partial distribution lock (most severe):** If distribute() is called with a partial batch that succeeds (e.g., distribute(5) processes holders[0..4]), LockedForDistribution becomes true and nextDistributionRecipient=5. On the next distribute() call, if holders[5] causes a revert, the transaction fails and the state remains: LockedForDistribution=true, nextDistributionRecipient=5. Since _beforeTokenTransfer checks require(!LockedForDistribution), ALL transfers/mints/burns are permanently blocked.

2. **Full distribution failure:** If distribute() always reverts at the same holder, LockedForDistribution reverts to false each time (since _beginDistribution and the revert happen in the same tx). But _isPastMinDistributionPeriod() remains true, so _beforeMintOrBurn() blocks all mints and burns permanently. Transfers still work but no distribution can ever complete.

Revert triggers include: USDT-like tokens that don't return bool (Solidity 0.8.x ABI decoder reverts on missing return data), blacklisted recipients (USDC/USDT blacklist), paused tokens, or insufficient balance from rebasing.

Partial recovery paths exist but are inadequate: admin can disapproveHolder() to skip a specific holder (losing their entitlement), or setDistributableERC20s([]) to skip all transfers (losing all remaining entitlements). Neither is documented, and for nonstandard tokens that revert for ALL recipients, disapproveHolder doesn't help.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 118-166 of LiquidInfrastructureERC20.sol confirm the vulnerable pattern. The transfer call at line ~147 uses raw IERC20.transfer() without try/catch or SafeERC20. Line 62 confirms LockedForDistribution blocks all token movements. No emergency unlock exists anywhere in the contract. The _beginDistribution() at line ~184 sets LockedForDistribution=true, and only _endDistribution() at line ~202 can set it back to false, but _endDistribution() requires nextDistributionRecipient==holders.length which cannot be reached if distribute() keeps reverting.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The IERC20.transfer() call is not wrapped in try/catch and does not use SafeERC20. If the external transfer reverts (blacklist, pause, nonstandard no-bool-return), the entire distribute() reverts, preventing nextDistributionRecipient from advancing. Combined with LockedForDistribution being true from a prior partial distribution call, this permanently blocks the contract.

This hook enforces require(!LockedForDistribution) for ALL token movements. Once LockedForDistribution is stuck as true (from a partial distribution that cannot complete), all transfers, mints, and burns are permanently blocked.

calling_sequence: 1. First call: distribute(5) processes holders[0..4] successfully
3. Loop processes 5 holders, transfers succeed, nextDistributionRecipient=5
5. Second call: distribute(1) attempts to process holders[5]
7. Loop enters, IERC20.transfer(holders[5], entitlement) reverts (blacklisted recipient)
8. Entire transaction reverts - nextDistributionRecipient stays at 5, LockedForDistribution stays true
9. All subsequent distribute() calls repeat step 5-8 and fail identically
10. _beforeTokenTransfer rejects all transfer/mint/burn attempts due to LockedForDistribution=true
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: distribute() -> _beginDistribution() sets LockedForDistribution=true -> outer loop iterates holders -> inner loop calls IERC20(distributableERC20s[j]).transfer(recipient, entitlement) -> if this external call reverts, entire tx reverts -> LockedForDistribution remains true from prior successful partial call -> _beforeTokenTransfer blocks all token movements -> no _endDistribution() ever called -> permanent lock

deployment_assumptions_checked: At least one distributableERC20 either (a) reverts on transfer for some recipients (blacklist/pause) or (b) is nonstandard (no boolean return) causing revert
At least one such recipient exists in holders[] and is approved at time of transfer attempt
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script uses TestERC20A as the distributable token with MinDistributionPeriod=10. Test tokens are standard ERC20s. The script does not deploy with USDT/USDC or blacklistable tokens. However, setDistributableERC20s can change the token list post-deployment, and the production token choice is not constrained by the test deployment.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is valid: (1) Attacker gets approved as holder via approveHolder, (2) Attacker receives tokens via mint, entering holders array, (3) A distributable token (e.g., USDC) blacklists the attacker's address, (4) Anyone initiates partial distribution that advances past some holders but stops before the attacker, (5) Next distribute() call reverts when transferring the blacklisted token to the attacker, (6) LockedForDistribution=true is permanent, freezing all contract operations. Alternative scenario: owner adds USDT (no bool return) as distributable token - ALL distribute() calls revert for ALL recipients, blocking mint/burn once MinDistributionPeriod elapses.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL: Protocol-wide freeze of all ERC20 transfer, mint, and burn operations. Revenue distributions cannot complete. No automated recovery mechanism exists. Admin workarounds (disapproveHolder, setDistributableERC20s) exist but cause entitlement losses and are undocumented. For nonstandard tokens like USDT, the issue affects ALL recipients, not just one, making disapproveHolder ineffective as a workaround.
recommended_mitigations: Use OpenZeppelin SafeERC20.safeTransfer() instead of IERC20.transfer() to handle nonstandard return values
Wrap each transfer in a try/catch block to gracefully skip failed transfers and emit a failure event
Add an admin emergencyUnlock() function that can reset LockedForDistribution to false and clear distribution state
Validate distributable ERC20s on addition (e.g., test transfer to self) to catch nonstandard tokens early
Consider a pull-based distribution model where holders claim their entitlements individually
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureERC20 with a mock blacklistable ERC20 as distributable token. 2. Approve and mint tokens to 3 holders. 3. Fund the contract with the distributable ERC20. 4. Advance blocks past MinDistributionPeriod. 5. Call distribute(1) to lock the contract and process holder[0]. 6. Blacklist holder[1] on the mock ERC20. 7. Call distribute(1) - expect revert. 8. Verify LockedForDistribution is still true. 9. Attempt a transfer - expect revert with 'distribution in progress'. 10. Repeat with USDT-like mock (no bool return) to show ALL recipients fail.
uncertainties: The admin CAN disapproveHolder() during a locked distribution to skip the problematic holder - this is a partial recovery path but causes entitlement loss
The admin CAN setDistributableERC20s([]) to skip all transfers and complete the distribution - this loses all remaining entitlements
The severity depends on whether production deployment uses blacklistable/nonstandard tokens
additional_testing_needed: Test with USDT-like mock (no bool return on transfer) to confirm ABI decoder revert in Solidity 0.8.12
Test partial distribution followed by blacklisting to confirm LockedForDistribution stays true
Test admin recovery path via disapproveHolder during locked state
Test admin recovery path via setDistributableERC20s([]) during locked state
Test with fee-on-transfer token to check if insufficient balance causes revert during later transfers
